data.title: YETI Holdings: Sell This Stock As U.S. Sales Falter And Tariffs Eat Into FCF (Downgrade)
data.description:
To me, a skyrocketing stock market continues to diverge drastically from the underlying weakness in the macroeconomy. Earnings results from consumer-facing companies alone help to attest to a slowdown in spending and confidence, and investors should take care to capitalize on recent market strength to lock in gains and wait for a much bigger dip before buying.

YETI Holdings (NYSE:YETI), in my view, is a company that is increasingly vulnerable. The outdoor products company has sharply cut its outlook for the year, making its valuation proposition shaky at best. It's pinning its hopes on international growth: but at the same time, overall margins are falling, hurt in no small part by tariffs. In spite of these headwinds, general market optimism has helped YETI to recover basically all of the losses it incurred after tariffs were announced, gaining ~30% from April lows in the mid-$20s. The question for investors now is: is YETI positioned for a rally, or is this a dead cat bounce?

I last wrote a neutral opinion on YETI in February, when the stock was trading near $38. Since then, YETI has fallen sharply on tariff news and then rebounded again: but at the same time, the company's pace of growth has slowed severely, coupled with meaningful losses in gross margin and overall profitability. Also notable is the fact that YETI has reduced its free cash flow guidance for FY25 by nearly half. With macroeconomic headwinds pressuring this company severely, I don't think YETI's rebound sufficiently prices in the added risks that the company faces, and I'm downgrading the stock to a sell rating.

With the guidance cut, YETI no longer looks cheap

Let's start with the number-one reason I'm pivoting to a bearish sentiment on YETI. Previously, the stock held some appeal as a value play, albeit one with a choppy track record for execution and slowing sales since the pandemic (especially after its rival Stanley took off in popularity).

That appeal has vanished, however, with YETI's deep guidance cut. The company is now only expecting 1-4% y/y adjusted sales growth this year (adjusted due to a 53-week fiscal year in FY25, versus a standard 52-week calendar in FY24) - indicating less than half of the company's previous growth rate expectations at 5-7% y/y. Making matters worse, when adjusting for its latest view on tariffs, YETI has also dropped its operating margin outlook to 12.0%, 490bps worse versus its prior outlook.

As a result, YETI's adjusted EPS guidance was also cut by -32% at the midpoint to $1.99, while FCF is now expected at $100-$125 million, or a -43% cut at the midpoint versus a prior $200 million expectation.

At current share prices near $37, YETI trades at a market cap of $3.05 billion. After we net off the $259.0 million of cash and $77.9 million of debt on YETI's latest balance sheet (or a $181 million net cash position that's worth ~6% of its current market value), the company's resulting enterprise value is $2.87 billion.

This positions YETI's valuation multiples at:

  • 18.5x FY25 P/E
  • 17.4x FY25 ex-cash P/E
  • 25.3x EV/FY25 FCF

For a company that is barely growing (and indeed, actually declining in the U.S.), these are not multiples I'm comfortable paying. The issue is that I don't view any of the current headwinds for YETI to be transitory in nature: and as such, I doubt that YETI shares will have the capacity to rebound meaningfully higher.

With U.S. sales falling, YETI's international sales are critical: but can growth last?

In YETI's latest quarter, sales in the U.S. declined -2% y/y to $271.3 million, or about 77% of the company's overall revenue. The only reason YETI was able to generate low single-digit revenue growth was via sharp growth in international, where revenue grew an impressive 22% y/y to $79.9 million.

International expansion is the core "upside risk" I see to my bear thesis on YETI, but the sustainability of this growth is shaky, in my view. YETI bulls will point to the fact that YETI has a relatively small share of overseas sales relative to other brands. The chart below showcases brands like Nike (NKE), with 57% of sales made overseas, alongside Deckers Outdoor (DECK) and Lululemon (LULU) in the mid/high 30s.

This sales profile suggests that, relative to YETI's current ~20% international revenue mix, the company could have the potential to double (or even greater) its current international revenue scale. As shown above, the company is currently only live in a few Western European countries, Canada, and the UK, and is planning on a broader-scale launch in Japan this year.

Here's what I view as the issue, however. Nike, Lululemon, Deckers, Under Armour (UA), and the other companies listed above are more generalized apparel makers. YETI, meanwhile, has a more specific outdoorsy-based product portfolio. In fact, one of its core signature products, the ice cooler, specifically gained popularity driven by the American football tailgate culture.

Yes, YETI also has general-appeal products like tumblers and camping cookware: certainly, the company can benefit from enthusiastic outdoorsmanship in European and Canadian markets. But as macro headwinds weigh on global consumer spending, and as trade tensions rise (leading to consumers in countries like Canada boycotting U.S. products), I have doubts that YETI will be able to sustain >20% growth internationally for a long period of time.

Tariffs will be a long-term drag on YETI's margins

The other headwind that deserves mention, beyond dragging U.S. sales, is YETI's exposure to tariffs. Over the past few years, YETI has indeed done a good job of diversifying its manufacturing supply chain outside of China. The company notes that on a go-forward basis, less than 5% of its total COGS will be exposed to U.S. tariffs on China.

That doesn't mean, however, that YETI has a get-out-of-jail-free card for its tariff bill. As shown in the global map above, YETI still has a large concentration of its supply chain in Asia: particularly in the Philippines, Indonesia (where President Trump recently announced trade deals amounting to a 19% headline tariff for both nations), and Vietnam, where the tariff rate is 20% (or 40% for goods that are deemed to be transshipments from China).

It's important to note that YETI's current outlook assumes only a 10% duty rate on non-Chinese nations (whereas the recent negotiations peg the major Southeast Asian nations at a ~20% tariff rate). Per CFO Mike McMullen's remarks on the Q1 earnings call in mid-May:

We now expect gross margins of approximately 54%, which is a decline of approximately 450 basis points versus both the prior year and our original FY2025 expectations. The primary driver of the decline is the impact of tariffs.

In this outlook, we are assuming that the following tariffs on products that we import into the U.S. are in place through the end of the year, a 145% total tariff rate on products sourced from China and a 10% reciprocal tariff rate on products sourced from all other countries where applicable to our business. Note that we are assuming that the current pause on higher reciprocal tariff rates on products sourced from non-China countries is extended to the end of 2025. Collectively, the gross amount of tariffs that are included in this outlook is approximately $100 million. That is before our mitigation efforts, which include pricing as well as cost offsets in partnership with our suppliers. Net of our mitigation efforts, the impact from tariffs that is included in this outlook is approximately 450 basis points."

Given the jump from 10% reciprocal tariff rates to ~20% in Southeast Asia, we expect YETI to further drop its outlook when it next releases earnings for Q2.

Key takeaways

To me, YETI faces a long list of risks: sluggish U.S. sales growth, the potential for deceleration in international sales growth, sharp tariff impacts that are eating into the company's margin profile and FCF, and a valuation that doesn't exactly leave much room for upside.

In my view, YETI's recent rebound rally is a great opportunity for investors to shed this stock and protect their capital: we'll have another chance to buy YETI down the road for a much cheaper price once updated tariff risks are fully priced in.

data.title: Baker Hughes: Data Center And LNG Optimism Builds (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Baker Hughes Overview

Shares of Baker Hughes (BKR) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 31%. These gains intensified Wednesday, with Baker adding about 10% thanks to extremely strong quarterly results and commentary. Baker Hughes continues to be a major beneficiary of increased US LNG activity, and it now is seeing increased benefits from data centers. I upgraded shares in June to “buy,” and since then, the stock has added 16%. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit BKR.

Cost Controls Support Baker Hughes' Q2 Results

In the company’s second quarter, Baker Hughes earned $0.63, which beat estimates by $0.08. While revenue was down 2.7% from last year at $6.9 billion, this was $300 million better than feared. Baker Hughes operates across two business lines: its legacy oil field services business and its growth unit focused on LNG and industrial power. Weak oil prices have weighed on its legacy business, while secular growth is powering new opportunities.

The combination of the two left BKR with modestly lower revenue than last year, but the company is generating significant operating efficiencies. Adjusted EBITDA grew by 7% to $1.2 billion as margins expanded by 170 bps from last year to 17.5% thanks to cost cuts. Indeed, BKR is generating efficiencies across segments. As a result, the company produced $239 million of free cash flow in the quarter.

IET Orders Remain Brisk With Added AI Upside

Looking into segment results, Industrial & Energy Technology (“IET”) revenue grew by 5% to $3.3 billion, and EBITDA margins of 17.8% expanded by 190 bps from last year. As a result, EBITDA grew by 18% to $585 million. This unit is the primary source of enthusiasm in shares and is what can make Baker Hughes a growth stock. There is tremendous investment along the Gulf Coast to increase our LNG export capacity, and given the large scale of these projects, this provides multiple years of revenue.

Indeed, as I have written about in the past, the primary challenge for Baker Hughes is scaling up its capacity to meet demand. It is supply, and not customer demand, that is capping how much revenue can grow. During the quarter, there were IET orders of $3.5 billion, up 2% from last year. This segment had a book to bill ratio of about 1.08x.

IET now has a backlog of $31.3 billion, providing over 2 years of future revenue. Especially with orders continuing to be higher than revenue, this provides a clear and predictable source of growth for the company. Importantly, while LNG-related activity remains the core source of the business, Baker Hughes is now seeing benefits from the AI-related data center booms, given these facilities need power and cooling capacity.

During the quarter, $550 million in power generation equipment orders from data centers. It aimed to achieve $1.7 billion of data center orders by the end of 2027, but it should exceed this. Baker Hughes now has agreements to supply 30 turbines powering data centers across the US. Again, these are multiyear projects, and importantly, initial equipment sales provide future, recurring service revenue, which tends to be higher margin. This creates a favorable cycle of recurring and growing revenue as its installed base increases. I would not view BKR as primarily an AI-infrastructure play. Rather, this boom just provides an additional lever for accelerating IET growth.

Cost Management In OFSE Impresses

The other side of the business is facing more challenges. Oilfield Services & Equipment (“OFSE”) revenue was $3.6 billion. This was up 3% from Q1, consistent with seasonal patterns. Impressively, despite the decline in sales, margins were 18.7%, leading to $677 million of EBITDA. Even with 10% lower revenue, margins expanded by 90 bps. We are clearly in an oil & gas downcycle, but BKR is managing through it extremely well.

Within OFSE, well construction revenue fell by 16% to $921 million, with completions also down by 16%. North America was down 9%, while Latin America was down just 4%. The Middle East is its most important region at $1.4 billion of revenue, and this was down 7%. With OPEC+ now increasing production, the Middle East should be nearing a bottom. Still, with more supply coming online and weak global demand growth, I struggle to see a surge in oil prices and expect OFSE activity to remain muted.

BKR Has A Strong Balance Sheet And Credible Guidance

Critically, Baker Hughes has an absolutely pristine balance sheet. It has $3.1 billion of cash and just $6 billion of debt, resulting in only 0.6x net debt to EBITDA leverage. Plus, portfolio optimization actions that I discussed in June should generate about $1 billion in cash. As a result, BKR has ample capacity to continue and even increase the pace of its capital returns. BKR did $196 million of buybacks in Q2, and its share count is down 1% from last year. BKR also has a 2% dividend yield, which I expect to grow annually.

Alongside results, management largely retained companywide guidance, but there were several shifts within guidance that point to the acceleration we are seeing in IET against the ongoing weakness of OFSE. It expects IET EBITDA to be $50 million better at $2.35 billion, but it maintained total company EBITDA of $4.675 billion, given pressure in its legacy OFSE business.

Management reiterated its view upstream oil & gas spending will decline high-single digits with North American activity underperforming. Management continues to expect full year IET orders of $12.5-$14.5 billion. Guidance also reflects the fact that Q2 had a $15 million tariff impact, and it sees $100 million of tariff costs in H2.

Given the orders in Q2 as well as acceleration in data center demand, I expect orders to come in the top-half of its IET guidance. Back in June, I saw the company delivering at the high end of its IET guidance while OFSE would decline by 10-13%. Management’s shifts in guidance essentially reflect my view that IET is strengthening and OFSE will be weaker for longer.

One upside risk over the medium term would be if President Trump can indeed get Asian partners to help finance a mega-LNG project in Alaska. Existing orders already support strong IET growth, but this project could provide meaningful further growth to BKR starting in 2027; I see no material impact before then. My view is that this project does not occur, but it could ultimately be a multi-billion opportunity for the company.

After The Rally, BKR Stock Is At Fair Value

Back in June, I was targeting about $2.40 +/- $.05 in EPS. This drove a fair value estimate of $42, which shares have now eclipsed. While I view the revenue picture as unchanged, BKR’s cost management in OFSE continues to exceed my own expectations, and so I now see EPS of $2.42-$2.52. I have viewed a ~17x multiple as appropriate, reflecting growth in IET and the slower-growth trajectory of OFSE.

Now, given the market’s focus on AI and some incremental lift from data centers, it is possible that BKR can sustain a higher multiple. Still, this is not a pure-play AI company by any means, the majority of revenue still comes from OFSE where no major revenue recovery is imminent. I would struggle to pay more than 18x, even reflecting data center upside, pointing to about $45-$46.

With shares having rallied 16% since my last article, I believe that the buy thesis has played out, occurring more quickly than I expected. With shares around fair value, I no longer view BKR as a “buy.” As such, I am downgrading shares to a “hold.” I do not see a rush to sell as BKR has an excellent balance sheet, is not overvalued, and has exposure to two key growth sectors: LNG & data centers. If we saw shares rally past $48, I would sell, and similarly, I would be a buyer if shares pull back to $40. At their current level, I expect BKR to be a market-performer going forward.

data.title: AT&T: Solid Second Quarter, More Upside Likely Long Term
data.description:
Introduction

Despite the challenging macro environment from higher for longer interest rates and now tariffs, telecom companies have performed well in the past year. AT&T (NYSE:T), once a Dividend Aristocrat, has been making headway after cutting their dividend a few years ago.

Their latest earnings report was solid, showing progress despite fiber subscribers missing the consensus, which resulted in a brief sell-off. But if the company can continue on its path, I think there's more upside to be had over the next year or two.

In this article, I discuss AT&T's latest performance, fundamentals, and why investors could see further upside, even after their strong price rally in the past year.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered AT&T back in February in an article titled: Strong Price Performance Justified With Potential For Further Upside.

Since, the stock has continued its rally, up over 12% compared to the S&P's (SP500) nearly 14.5% in the past 5 months.

AT&T's strong rally was justified by their improved efficiencies, debt reduction, and announced buyback program.

As a result, I rated the stock a buy, as they also offered significant upside to their price target of nearly $34 if the stock could return to its historical valuation.

Solid Quarter

AT&T reported their Q2 earnings and managed to beat on both their top and bottom lines despite fewer fiber subscribers than the Bloomberg consensus. EPS of $0.54 beat by a penny, while their top line beat by a solid $370 million.

Revenue amounted to $30.8 billion. As a result, both their top and bottom lines grew 3.4% and 5.9%, respectively, from the previous year. For comparison, peer Verizon (VZ) saw slightly higher growth in both metrics year-over-year.

VZ's revenue increased 5.2% from $32.8 billion to $34.5 billion, while earnings per share increased 6.1% from $1.15 to $1.19.

AT&T's performance was driven by higher net income and higher mobility & consumer wireline revenues. Net income increased from $3.9 billion to $4.9 billion. Postpaid phone net adds were 401,000, while the aforementioned fiber subscriber net additions amounted to 243,000, falling below the 252,000 consensus.

Consumer fiber broadband revenues grew an impressive 18.9% to $2.1 billion from the prior year. The company also divested their remaining 70% stake in DirecTV during the quarter.

Growing Cash Flows

When it comes to investing in telecom companies, growing cash flows and deleveraging are important metrics investors like to see. During the quarter, T's free cash flow increased from $4 billion to $4.4 billion, a result of higher cash from operations, which was up $700 million to $9.8 billion.

Moreover, T expects to recognize cash savings between $1.5 billion and $2 billion this year. In the next two years, they expect this to increase to $2.5 billion to $3 billion.

As far as free cash flow growth, management is anticipating this is a range of $16+ billion this year, a decline from the previous year's $17.6 billion. But in 2026 and beyond, T is expecting to get back on the path to free cash flow growth with $18+ billion in 2026 and $19+ billion in 2027.

If they can achieve this, this would give them more flexibility for continuous share repurchases. Furthermore, this would likely drive earnings growth, leading to upside potential over the long term.

Balance Sheet

The company also continued to make a dent in its balance sheet. Net debt stood at $120.3 billion, while total debt was $132.3 billion. For comparison, Verizon's net debt was slightly lower at $116 billion and total debt of $146 billion.

T's debt declined from the previous year's quarter's $126.9 billion and $130.6 billion, respectively. Verizon's decreased from $122.8 billion and $149.3 billion, respectively. T's leverage stood at 2.63x compared to VZ's 2.3x.

Cash & equivalents amounted to $10.5 billion. Continued debt reduction and share repurchases will also likely drive further price appreciation. So far this year, management has bought back $1.3 billion shares and $1 billion worth in Q2 alone. For 2025, they plan to repurchase $4 billion worth.

Well-Covered Dividend

As previously mentioned, AT&T's free cash flow amounted to $4.4 billion during the quarter and $7.5 billion for the first half, with $4.1 billion in dividends paid.

As a result, AT&T's free cash flow payout ratio sits at 54.6%. This was notably lower than Verizon's 64.7%, as their free cash flow for the first half of 2025 amounted to $8.8 billion, with $5.7 billion paid out in the form of dividends.

This gives T ample room for further buyback implementations and dividend increases. Additionally, there's a good chance shareholders could see a dividend increase in the near future, which could also increase investors' confidence and drive price appreciation since the dividend has remained flat since 2022.

2025 Outlook

Although they didn't raise their guidance like Verizon, T's outlook for the year remains solid with strong Consumer Fiber Broadband revenue growth of double-digits. EPS, like free cash flow, is expected to decline from $2.26 the previous year. But beyond 2025, T looks to accelerate growth, which could lead to further upside in the next year or two.

More Upside Likely In The Next 12 To 24 Months

Despite negative growth this year, T looks poised for solid growth ahead with an expected growth rate of 5.7% over the next 5 years. If so, I expect their share price to follow, especially since interest rates will likely be lower by then.

Using the Discounted Cash Flow Method, T's upside from the current price of $27.47 is appealing with a price target of $36 a share. I use a 4% growth rate afterwards to account for their mature business and an 11% expected return, slightly above the S&P's historical 7%–10% due to their higher yield and lower growth.

Risks & Conclusion

One risk that could impact AT&T is the FED's decision regarding interest rates. While many still expect two rate cuts this year, tariffs could impact this, leading to a potential sell-off in the sector as a result of their high debt loads.

Investors should watch this closely and look to add on any share price volatility in the coming months. Moreover, the upcoming August 1st deadline could also result in a sell-off, although this could change at any time.

If further tariffs are implemented and these prove to be inflationary, T's segments could see further weakening as consumers remain financially constrained as the FED will likely continue to keep rates elevated.

But if you're a long-term investor, these should be viewed as opportunities, as I view T's low forward multiple of 13.59x as attractive.

As a result of this, their expected cost savings, free cash flow growth, and share buybacks, I think AT&T is poised to see further upside in the next 1 to 2 years, making them a buy at the current price.

data.title: Eurozone Bank Lending Growth Strengthens Despite Economic Uncertainty
data.description:
By Bert Colijn

Bank lending growth accelerated again in June, indicating that global economic uncertainty is not significantly dampening lending at this time

Bank lending growth to the private sector has recently shown signs of slowing, with May experiencing an outright decline month-on-month in terms of lending volumes to non-financial corporates. But a strong rebound in June has eased concerns that uncertainty about the global economy – and tariffs in particular – was hindering proper monetary transmission.

But the view is more nuanced than that. The data is volatile, and taking a three-month average, we do still note some moderation in bank lending growth. However, while uncertainty dampens investment appetite, there is no clear sign that demand for credit is falling quickly. In fact, the year-on-year growth rates that the ECB reports in its press release are still increasing. And the ECB bank lending survey, out earlier this week, also indicated that borrowing appetite remains decent.

The ECB, having kept rates on hold yesterday, will happily take more information about this uncertain economy on board ahead of the September meeting. During this meeting, the debate about whether to implement another rate cut will be back on the table. More data on bank lending will come in before then, which may provide a clearer view on whether uncertainty impairs monetary transmission or not. For now, the picture isn't too alarming.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Portfolio Continuum: Rethinking Gold In Alternatives Investing
data.description:
Executive summary

The rise of alternative assets in institutional portfolios has been driven by the pursuit of enhanced returns, broader diversification, and insulation from traditional market cycles. Yet, these benefits often come with trade-offs – limited liquidity, delayed valuations, and exposure to economic shocks that may not be fully priced in real-time.

Building on our previous research, this paper explores how gold can serve as a complementary asset within alternative allocations. While not always classified as an ‘alternative’, gold offers distinct advantages: it is highly liquid, lowly correlated to other asset classes, and tends to perform well during periods of systemic stress, with its inclusion in a portfolio acting as a shock absorber.

We also look into private credit, a fast-growing corner of alternatives. While fundamentals remain supportive and institutional investor interest strong, the asset class still carries familiar challenges around liquidity and valuation lags. Gold enhances downside resilience and provides capital flexibility across alternative asset strategies when distributions slow or exit timelines extend.

To investors, public and private markets exist along a continuum of liquidity, returns, and volatility. The difference is one of timing and access. Gold exists in this continuum, not because it mimics public or private assets, but because its attributes bridge across both.

Introduction

Alternative assets have reshaped modern portfolios. Assets under management (AUM) for alternative assets is projected to reach close to $30 trillion by 2029, with private equity projected to double in AUM to $12 trillion by then.1 The premise for allocation into alternative assets rests upon a few key ideas:

  • Diversification
  • Lower correlations to traditional assets
  • Enhanced returns of existing portfolios

Building on our previous research that highlights gold as a foundational diversifier within alternative portfolios, we continue the discussion on gold's interplay with alternative strategies, including the growing role of private credit.

Gold’s correlation dynamics with alternatives

While equity-bond correlations have by and large been negative over the past decade, it has turned positive since 2022.2 Institutional investors have increasingly shifted their allocations into alternative assets to complement the traditional equity-bond framework for diversification.

An asset class such as gold plays a role within this framework. Its historical status as a safe-haven asset and store of value offers a layer of diversification. Over the medium- to long-term horizon, gold tends to have a low correlation to alternative assets (Chart 1). For hedge funds, we note an increasing correlation as the investment horizon increases. The reason for this, perhaps, is the liquidity profile of hedge funds. Hedge funds, particularly those with liquid trading strategies, generally have allocations into assets that can be manoeuvred as market conditions change.

Chart 1: Gold’s correlation with alternatives remains low over time

A consistent diversifier, gold exhibits declining correlation to most alternative asset across 3-, 5- and 15-year horizons

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

Data as of 30 May 2025.

Indices used S&P 500 Total Return Index, MSCI Emerging Markets Total Return Index, Refinitiv Private Equity Buyout Index, Bloomberg Global Aggregate Total Return Index, Credit Suisse Hedge Fund Index, FTSE NAREIT Equity REITs Total Return Index, ICE BofA 3-Month US Treasury Bill Index, LBMA Gold Price PM (USD/oz), S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan Total Return Index

Within hedge funds, global macro and trend-following commodity trading advisors (CTAs) increase gold exposure as its momentum strengthens. Private equity, listed real estate and private credit, on the contrary, have a very different return and liquidity profile to gold. For example, the performance of private credit is generally tied to yields and corporate health. Like most private investments, it comes with a long-term commitment and is less liquid.

Correlations between public and private equities have been high and have increased over the medium to long term due to the fund lifecycle. As the fund cycle matures, private equity valuations tend to align more closely with those of public markets. The underlying portfolio companies are revalued more frequently as exit strategies, such as IPOs, become clearer. As such, valuations align more with prevailing market conditions, pushing correlation higher around the 10-year mark—before easing slightly at 15 years as funds wind down and remaining assets become less market-sensitive (Chart 2).

Chart 2: Private equity becomes more correlated with public markets over time

As private equity funds mature, valuations reflect public market conditions, peaking in correlation around the 10-year mark

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

Data as of 30 May 2025.

Indices used Refinitiv Private Equity Buyout Index, LBMA Gold Price PM (USD/oz)

Periods of market stress – like the Global Financial Crisis and COVID-19 pandemic – are when diversification is tested. A key attribute of gold is its role as liquidity insurance during times of crisis. Fundraising, deal activity, and exits all slow during these periods, putting pressure on private market cash flows. To manage this, General Partners (GPs) had to innovate — whether through secondary sales or continuation funds, to keep capital redistribution schedules on track. These structures help manage liquidity at the fund level but do not eliminate the underlying challenge: capital remains locked for longer, and access to cash is less predictable. Gold, by contrast, offers immediacy and flexibility — accessible even when other parts of the portfolio are illiquid.

Chart 3: Gold’s resilience in market stress

Gold held up better during shocks, with returns staying within 1–2 standard deviations

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

Data as of 30 May 2025

Indices used Refinitiv Private Equity Buyout Index, LBMA Gold Price PM (USD/oz). Shock periods: Global Financial Crisis (1 Oct – 31 Dec 2008), COVID-19 (1 Jan – 31 Mar 2020), 2018 Fed Tightening (1 Oct – 31 Dec 2018), Eurozone Debt Crisis (1 Jul – 30 Sep 2011)

We examined gold and private equity performance across four historical liquidity events: the 2008 Global Financial Crisis, the COVID shock in early 2020, the Q4 2018 credit squeeze, and the Eurozone debt crisis in 2011 (Chart 3). In these four events, gold performance stayed within one to two standard deviations of its long-term distribution, indicating relatively contained downside risk. By contrast, private equity returns were impacted in all four events, with delayed losses due to valuation lags and limited liquidity. This comparison highlights gold’s potential to respond more promptly and resiliently to market stress.

Gold: a complementary asset

The performance of alternative assets depends on several factors, including the deal and exit environment, leverage terms, and the redistribution schedules of Limited Partners (LPs). While their diversification benefits help mitigate portfolio volatility to some extent, they often come with liquidity constraints and valuation challenges, requiring a long-term commitment to fully capture their risk-adjusted return potential.

Table 1 shows a typical diversified portfolio3 optimised using Monte Carlo simulations (see Appendix for methodology). Based on historical data of the past 20 years, the simulation indicates that the optimal allocation to gold rests between 5% and 8%. This would have helped improve risk-adjusted returns and reduce volatility. In this simulation, the alternative assets account for about one-quarter of the total portfolio.

Table 1: Adding gold improves portfolio efficiency across timeframes

Data as of 30 May 2025

Indices used S&P 500 Total Return Index, MSCI Emerging Markets Total Return Index, Refinitiv Private Equity Buyout Index, Bloomberg Global Aggregate Total Return Index, Credit Suisse Hedge Fund Index, FTSE NAREIT Equity REITs Total Return Index, ICE BofA 3-Month US Treasury Bill Index, LBMA Gold Price PM (USD/oz), S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan Total Return Index.

Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council

Chart 4: How an allocation to gold cushions portfolios

In simulated scenarios, gold stems portfolio losses by 50-90bps

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

Data as of 30 May 2025

Indices used S&P 500 Total Return Index, MSCI Emerging Markets Total Return Index, Refinitiv Private Equity Buyout Index, Bloomberg Global Aggregate Total Return Index, Credit Suisse Hedge Fund Index, FTSE NAREIT Equity REITs Total Return Index, ICE BofA 3-Month US Treasury Bill Index, LBMA Gold Price PM (USD/oz), S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan Total Return Index.

We also tested our optimised portfolio under four macroeconomic stress scenarios4 – equity crash, inflation spike, rate hike shock, and credit spread widening – to evaluate its resilience across distinct sources of systemic risk (see Appendix 2). While no two investor portfolios are identical, this approach anchors our analysis in real-world dynamics by mapping historical relationships between asset classes and macro stress factors.

These assumptions are based on historical stress periods and market patterns. The simulated portfolio results highlight gold’s potential as a risk mitigator across diverse macro shocks. In every scenario – be it a rate hike, inflation spike, equity crash or credit stress – adding gold reduced the portfolio’s drawdown by 50 to 90 basis points. And while modest, this consistency underscores gold’s role as a portfolio stabiliser, mainly when traditional and alternative assets come under simultaneous pressure.

Gold and credit strategies

An alternative asset class of recent interest is private credit, or lending strategies originating outside of traditional banking channels. Private credit strategies span a broad spectrum – from senior secured lending to opportunistic and distressed debt – allowing investors to tailor exposure based on return targets and risk tolerance. The trade-off, however, is illiquidity and less frequent revaluation, which can obscure and dampen volatility in times of stress.

The asset class has gained prominence—initially in the low-interest rate environment as investors searched for yield, and more recently amid ongoing regulatory shifts, as banks deleverage their balance sheets in response to Basel IV.5 What this means for private credit is the potential expansion of certain types of loans, which were previously parked under the banks’ balance sheets; for instance, specialty finance, agriculture, and small-to-medium enterprise loans. As banks deleverage, private credit investors could scoop up these loans and negotiate favourable terms. And while this regulation may impact Europe in the mid-term, investors with global private credit mandates should continue to look at this asset class with interest.

Understanding underlying credit markets is essential, as private credit is fundamentally exposed to the same credit cycle dynamics6 – just with more opacity and lag. The ICE BofA US High Yield Bond Index returned 8% in 2024 while the LSTA Leveraged Loans Index returned 9% in the same year. Both are useful distant proxies for understanding private credit’s risk-return dynamics. These indices reflect similar underlying borrower profiles – sub-investment grade corporates – and comparable exposure to credit and macro conditions, albeit in the public markets.

A typical private credit portfolio combines elements of capital preservation and yield enhancement—often through a mix of senior secured lending and opportunistic strategies. When gold is added to such a portfolio, risk-adjusted returns improve. Gold acts as a liquidity buffer and risk management tool, especially valuable during credit market dislocations when traditional hedges, like government bonds, may be less reliable (Chart 5).

Chart 5: Gold enhances the risk-return profile of private credit portfolios7

Gold allocation provides downside protection when credit markets come under pressure

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

Data as of 31 March 2025

Signs of credit stress

While investor appetite for private credit continues to grow, developments in the broader leveraged finance market – particularly Collateralised Loan Obligations (CLOs) can offer a forward view into stress transmission that may eventually surface in private portfolios. This strengthens the case for holding liquid buffers, like gold, to manage funding needs and portfolio shocks when private asset valuations adjust with a lag.

While CLOs and private credit differ structurally, they share a similar borrower base. CLO signals – such as rising CCC-rated exposure or shrinking Junior Overcollateralization (OC) cushions – can serve as early-warning indicators for credit deterioration. These metrics are handy for private credit investors, where pricing lags and transparency are limited.

Chart 6: CLOs reveal early signs of credit stress through rising CCC exposures

CCC-rated loans breaching the 7.5% limit can signal credit stress ahead of private credit markdowns

Sources: Bloomberg; Disclaimer

Data as of 31 March 2025

In early 2020, as the COVID-19 shock roiled markets, CCC-rated exposures in CLOs spiked sharply. This triggered breaches in Junior Overcollateralisation tests and compressed subordination cushions, surfacing signs of credit stress well before they appeared in private credit valuations.

Pitchbook8 data showed private lending funds were down 6.2% in the first quarter of 2020 – the worst quarterly performance since the Global Financial Crisis – and performance remained flat in the second quarter. Yet, unlike public credit, these markdowns were contained, partly due to lagged quarterly Net Asset Value (NAV) reporting. The asset class eventually recovered in part due to central bank stimulus and active support from sponsors, including liquidity injections and operational support.

So where does gold fit in all this? It may not offer the outsized return potential of private credit – or any private market investment for that matter – but it brings a set of attributes that are increasingly hard to ignore:

A shock absorber, as seen in our stress-tested portfolio. Gold tends to respond immediately to market dislocations, preserving value when drawdowns in other assets, including private credit, take time to surface.

A source of liquidity, providing flexibility when capital calls spike or investor redemptions loom. It gives managers breathing room, allowing them to hold on to long-term positions without forced sales.

A diversification asset, especially in periods when traditional diversifiers, from hedge funds to real estate, exhibit rising correlations.

In short, gold complements private investments by addressing their blind spots.

What lies ahead

Deals and exits are the cornerstone of private markets – they determine where capital is put to work and how LPs realise return potential. Charts 7 and 8 tell a consistent story: both deal counts and volumes are trending lower. Once a reliable path to liquidity (and returns), IPOs have lost momentum, contributing to slower exits. Subdued mergers and acquisitions (M&A) activity reflects a more challenging environment for deal-making.

Chart 7: Global M&A activity on the down trend10

Deal activity has slowed amid tighter credit and valuation uncertainty

Sources: Bloomberg; Disclaimer

Data as of 31 March 2025

Chart 8: Last 12-month IPO signal cooling in exit momentum11

IPO activity has declined over the past 12 months, contributing to the liquidity bottleneck in private markets

Sources: Bloomberg; Disclaimer

Data as of 31 March 2025

Today, innovation is reshaping exit strategies. The emergence of continuation funds or GP-led secondaries reflects a need to generate liquidity, either via secondary sales or by rolling assets into new vehicles to provide an additional lifeline. In 2024, GP-led secondary volume accounted for 46% of total secondary market activity – almost on par with traditional LP-led secondary activity. For context, GP-led secondaries were at 24% back in 2016.9

In practice, investors do not view public and private markets as binary choices but as points along a continuum of liquidity, returns, and volatility. Gold exists in this continuum, not because it mimics public or private assets, but because its attributes cut across both. It trades with the liquidity of public markets yet behaves with the defensive stability investors often seek in private strategies. In portfolios balancing public and private allocations, gold becomes a natural bridge. As portfolios are tested across cycles, gold provides the quiet strength that holds the foundation in place.

Appendix

Portfolio Simulation and Efficient Frontier

To evaluate the role of gold within a multi-asset portfolio, we conducted a Monte Carlo simulation comprising 10,000 portfolios. Each portfolio was constructed using monthly log return data from January 2000 to May 2025 across the following asset classes: developed and emerging market equities, private equity, hedge funds, REITs, leveraged loans, fixed income, gold, and cash.

Simulation Methodology

Portfolios were generated under the following allocation constraints:

Equities (Developed + Emerging): Capped at 45%

Alternatives via proxy (Private Equity, Hedge Funds, REITs, Loans): Capped at 25%

Fixed Income and Cash: Remaining allocation

Gold: Allowed to vary

No short positions were taken — all asset weights were constrained to be positive. This simplifies the analysis, and to some extent reflects long-only mandates, especially in asset owner portfolios.

Each portfolio’s annualised return, volatility, and Sharpe ratio were calculated. The efficient frontier below shows the resulting portfolios plotted by annualised return versus volatility. Although individual asset weights vary, higher Sharpe ratio portfolios tend to cluster around moderate volatility levels, reflecting a favourable risk-return trade-off.

Chart 9: Risk-adjusted returns on simulated portfolios

Sources: Bloomberg, World Gold Council; Disclaimer

Chart 10: Average gold weight per Sharpe ratio bucket

Data as of 30 May 2025.

Indices used S&P 500 Total Return Index, MSCI Emerging Markets Total Return Index, Refinitiv Private Equity Buyout Index, Bloomberg Global Aggregate Total Return Index, Credit Suisse Hedge Fund Index, FTSE NAREIT Equity REITs Total Return Index, ICE BofA 3-Month US Treasury Bill Index, LBMA Gold Price PM (USD/oz), S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan Total Return Index.

Stress Testing the Portfolio

To complement the portfolio optimisation analysis, we conducted stress tests across four macroeconomic scenarios to evaluate the resilience of gold-inclusive portfolios under systemic shocks. The scenarios chosen reflect key risk environments relevant to both traditional and alternative asset classes.

We modelled four stress scenarios:

Rate Hike Shock – based on rapid policy tightening (e.g., 2022 Fed cycle)

Inflation Spike – based on post-COVID inflation surprises

Equity Crash – referencing episodes like the COVID drawdown (Q1 2020)

Credit Spread Widening – mirroring HY market dislocations (e.g., late 2018)

Simplifying the analysis, this is a scenario-based first-order stress test - if event X happens (e.g., an equity crash), then the portfolio returns changes by Y (See Footnote 4 for list of shocks applied in the model).

We also assessed the portfolio’s downside risk using historical Value-at-Risk (VaR). This estimates potential monthly loss in tail-risk events by examining actual historical returns distributions. We calculated historical VaR at 95% and 99% confidence levels for our optimised portfolio and a portfolio excluding gold by proportionally scaling up the weights of the remaining asset class to maintain a 100% allocation.

Table 2: Gold’s inclusion lowered extreme monthly losses by 77bp at 95% VaR and 51bp at 99% VaR

Data as of 30 May 2025.

Indices used S&P 500 Total Return Index, MSCI Emerging Markets Total Return Index, Refinitiv Private Equity Buyout Index, Bloomberg Global Aggregate Total Return Index, Credit Suisse Hedge Fund Index, FTSE NAREIT Equity REITs Total Return Index, ICE BofA 3-Month US Treasury Bill Index, LBMA Gold Price PM (USD/oz), S&P/LSTA Leveraged Loan Total Return Index.

Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council.

Footnotes

1Alternatives AUM tipped to cross US$30 trillion by 2030 | The Asset

2Alternative diversifiers: Rethinking diversification in investment portfolios

3Under an optimised portfolio, the allocation is as follows: 45% developed and emerging market equities, 24% alternatives, 23% fixed income and 8% gold. This portfolio consists of S&P 500 Total Return Index, MSCI Emerging Market Total Return Index, Refinitiv Private Equity Buyout Index, Bloomberg Global Aggregate Total Return Index, Credit Suisse Hedge Fund Index, FTSE NAREIT Equity REITs Index Total Return, S&P Leveraged Loan Index, ICE BofA 3-month US Treasury Bill Index, and LBMA Gold Price PM USD Index. Based on data from January 2000 to May 2025. Proportional reduction in allocation to other asset classes with increasing gold allocation.

4Stress-scenario simulations are based on assumptions of how key asset classes might behave under different macroeconomic environments. Rate Hike Shock assumes a 100bps increase in interest rates, leading to broad declines in equities (–7%) and global bonds (–4%), while gold rises modestly (+2%). Inflation Spike models a 1–2% rise in y-o-y CPI, resulting in equity pullbacks (–3.5%), mild bond weakness (–2%), and a stronger gold response (+5%). Equity Crash simulates a –20% drop in equities, alongside a rally in sovereign bonds (+3%) and gold (+10%) on flight-to-safety demand. Credit Spread Widening reflects a broad risk-off move in credit markets, with private equity (–4%), leveraged loans (–5%), and global aggregate bonds (–3%) under pressure as spreads widen, while gold gains (+2%) and cash outperforms. These assumptions are not forecasts but are used illustratively to test portfolio sensitivity under plausible macro conditions. Based on data from January 2000 to March 2025

5Growing investment opportunities amid bank deleveraging trend

6Howard Marks: Gimme Credit, March 2025

7Portfolio constructed using publicly available proxies for private credit exposure: Leveraged Loans (SPBDAL Index), High Yield Bonds (ICE BofA HY Index), and Cash (G0O1 Index). Gold allocation varies from 0% to 15%, offset proportionally from leveraged loan exposure. All returns are monthly total returns converted to log returns. Portfolio returns are calculated as a weighted sum of asset returns, with risk-adjusted performance measured by the Sharpe ratio (using 12-month rolling average)

8PitchBook_2020_Annual_Global_Private_Debt_Report.pdf

91H2022-Jefferies-Global-Secondary-Market-Review.pdf

10Bloomberg

11Bloomberg

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Cipher Mining: A Leveraged Bet On Bitcoin's Bull Cycle
data.description:
Cipher Mining Inc. (NASDAQ:CIFR) is a rapidly growing bitcoin extraction company that can significantly increase its income due to an increasing hash rate and more efficient equipment in the coming years. Although the company is not yet profitable, the capital structure is relatively healthy, and the development pace is based on the price of Bitcoin; if it remains at a high level or continues to rise, Cipher can quickly reach positive cash flows in the coming years. For these reasons, I believe this company is worth a look and can be considered a speculative buy for exposure to cryptocurrency through the company, not the coins themselves.

Financial Health

So we have rapid growth, but at the same time, structural challenges to profitability and efficiency. Over the last twelve months, the company generated $152.09 million in revenue, but the net loss was $123.51 million. This is due not only to high fixed costs but also to the structure of the activity not being optimized just yet.

The gross profit was $27.59 million, which means the gross margin was ~18.1%. The EBITDA was around $9.72 million, and the operating income was -$118.95 million. This indicates that the company is not yet able to achieve a positive operating profit. EPS sits at -$0.37, which demonstrates a direct loss to shareholders.

What about balance and liquidity? The company's cash is $23.17 million, while the total debt is $58.38 million. Net debt is $35.21 million. The current ratio is 1.11, which indicates that the company is currently capable of covering short-term obligations but has restricted reserves. Quick ratio is just 0.41, which means that if we take out the reserves, liquid measures are not sufficient to cover short-term liabilities. The company's equity is relatively strong—the book value per share is $1.98. The debt-to-equity ratio is 7.95%, a low level of leverage.

From a liquidity point of view, Cipher currently has about $23 million in cash when operating losses and capital costs remain significant. With such a net level and maintaining a similar money-burning rate, the company should have runway for the next 2-3 quarters, especially if the capital investment in new equipment decreases. However, the need for additional funding remains real in the third and fourth quarters of 2025, unless the price of BTC increases significantly and improves the flows. So far, Cipher has successfully attracted capital from emissions, indicating some institutional trust.

Summary. Cipher Mining's financial structure is moderately healthy—the company has little long-term debt and a certain liquidity, but high losses and negative cash flows from operating activities pose a risk to sustainability in the long term. Fixed costs, improving profitability indicators, and positive cash flows are required to maintain the operations and achieve profitability. Although the structure of the capital is not dangerous yet, the dependence on external funding indicates a higher risk of sensitivity to market changes and BTC prices.

Catalysts

Cipher is approaching a significant hash rate jump—from the current ~13.5 EH/s to the planned 23 EH/s at the end of 2025. Such growth is not just symbolic—it could mean that the company's BTC extraction volumes increase by more than 60%. Bearing in mind that the results of the last quarter alone have been based on older generation equipment, new equipment implementation (like Canaan Avalon A1466) may mean a direct rise in income with a lower cost. This is especially important at current BTC prices.

By increasing capacity, Cipher can break down fixed costs on a wider hash rate basis. This means that even at stable BTC prices, the company's profitability potential is improving structurally. Improving operating efficiency is often a long-term investor's main driver, and Cipher shows real progress here.

Demand recovery in the BTC market after the correction of 2022-2023. In the middle of 2025, the price stabilized, but historically, such levels are only temporary. If the price of BTC surged, Cipher could generate positive EBITDA even without further development. It becomes an interesting speculative lever for the BTC price cycle.

Cipher operates in Texas, a state famous for business-friendly conditions and access to abundant energy. This means that, even with the rise in electricity prices on a global scale, Cipher can maintain a low production cost. In addition, the company's orientation to renewable sources allows it to be less vulnerable to future ESG requirements.

In 2024-2025, the company successfully distributed new shares and attracted over $170 million. Such issuance and demand indicate that institutional investors consider Cipher's growth. If this confidence persists and a new partnership or additional funding can be seen, it can reduce the pressure on cash flows.

Black Pearl and other projects show not only geographical expansion but also strategic thinking of the management team. Diversifying infrastructure across several places reduces systemic risk and makes it less vulnerable to technical or logistical disorders.

Cipher Mining is currently operating in the growth phase, but most importantly, this growth is efficiency-based and linked to fundamental activity improvement indicators. Both capacity increases and efficiency growth through new equipment, and the stability of the public environment, create the basis for better future financial results.

Risks

We do have here some great catalysts, but risks are major as well.

The highest risk is related to the volatility of the cryptocurrency market. Cipher's profitability depends directly on the price of Bitcoin, and this market has high fluctuations. The correction of 2022-2023 has shown that the entire EBITDA level can be lost within months. Even if the company reduces the cost, the current hash rate capacity may turn into losses when the BTC price falls.

Although Texas currently has a competitive advantage over low energy prices, it is not guaranteed in the long term. Electricity prices or regulatory changes in the state can significantly increase production costs and reduce margins.

The company currently generates negative free cash flows, which means that its operations depend on the additional capital attraction. If the market conditions change, the company may face a liquidity problem. There was also a moment where they attracted new capital with share issuance, so dilution for investors might be another risk.

Because Cipher is expanding rapidly, every technological delay, equipment failure, or supply chain disorder can have a disproportionate effect. The BTC extraction business is sensitive to time – every day lost reduces overall efficiency. It is also a significant risk – the transition to newer equipment can be more expensive or will take longer than planned due to unexpected reasons.

The cryptocurrency sector remains in a regulatory uncertainty area. New taxes, bans, or reporting requirements can significantly increase operating costs.

Valuation

When valuating Cipher Mining, the company currently seems to be cheap to a cyclical investor, but the risk profile remains highly dependent on results.

EV/Sales are about 1.5x, which is significantly less than most technological or even among bitcoin mining sector players, with EV/S often exceeding 3-4x. The P/S ratio is about 2x, moderate, given the potential growth and the planned 60% rate extension. However, the valuation of EV/EBITDA remains distorted due to negative profit rates, so the discounted cash flow remains speculative and the share price may fluctuate completely in response to news, BTC movement, and not fundamentals.

Compared to competitors such as Riot or Marathon Digital, CIFR looks cheaper but also riskier. However, with the rise in BTC prices, this leverage can turn into a value catalyst. Good for those with a tolerance for volatility.

If Cipher succeeds in increasing capacity to 23 EH/s and achieves historical operational efficiency (~$60-65 TH/s), the annual income could approach $300-350 million, depending on the price of BTC (all in hopes it goes up). With ~35-40% of total profitability and increasing efficiency, it is possible to achieve about 90-100 million EBITDA a year.

In this case, the EV/EBITDA multiplier would decrease to ~7x levels, which would be lower than most mature BTC companies selling at 8-10x. If the price of BTC exceeded $100,000, EBITDA could increase over $120 million, reducing EV/EBITDA to 5-6x, which would be very attractive to investors looking for a leap of cyclical profitability.

Summary

Cipher Mining is currently not operating as a mature player but as a cyclic growth player with a real opportunity to expand its revenue base exponentially in the coming years. Although the profitability of the company remains negative, especially at EBITDA and at the net profit level, the company is just completing its infrastructure base. It's like investing in a junior gold miner, which is buying its first drill.

The main catalysts are hash rate growth, the installation of new equipment, the ability to reduce the cost per BTC, the favorable Texas environment, and the institutional injection of capital—all these indicate that Cipher's Board thinks about the long-term scale effect and systemic efficiency. If the price of the BTC surges, the company could achieve a positive EBITDA even without additional money. And the development up to 23 EH/s by the end of 2025, theoretically, could double the BTC extraction.

However, a dependence on BTC cycles, high activity sensitivity to costs, and negative free cash flows mean that Cipher is still a "high-risk, high-return" investment. This is not a dividend, conservative portfolio player—more like a leveraged way to get into the BTC market with the possibility of gaining a lot if all parts fall into place.

The valuation is currently not discouraging, and the institutional demand for newly issued shares indicates that the company is not left behind. Given this cycle, current multiplicators may appear ridiculously low. Therefore, as I am an investor with good tolerance for volatility, this stock is a good choice for a speculative buy, but due to the nature of crypto, it's not a long-term investment.

data.title: AppLovin's Ad-Tech Playbook Is Gaining Traction
data.description:
At first glance, AppLovin's (NASDAQ:APP) AI-first, performance-driven pivot might look like just another short-term rebound. However, the multi-year evolution from a hybrid model focused on software and gaming to a more focused advertising business, powered by proprietary machine learning models built in-house, is starting to exhibit structural lift.

At a time when investors are pulling back from short-lived successes and becoming cautious about unclear ad networks, AppLovin's careful spending and steady investment in its Axon AI system challenge the idea that it's just another ad-tech company benefiting from broad market trends.

This analysis explores why the market still might be underestimating the stickiness in its performance-driven model, the sustainability in its EBITDA leverage, as well as the upside in its upcoming foray into CTV and general web advertising, even as short sellers gather, raising concerns about sustainability.

Axon's Evolving Engine: Why Reinforcement Learning is the Core Growth Driver

The shift to an AI-driven platform is no window dressing for AppLovin. The company's most recently released earnings show how its Axon model functions less like a traditional ad network and more like a self-improving revenue engine. The headline numbers, 71% year-over-year growth in advertising revenue to $1.16 billion and a 92% surge in advertising adjusted EBITDA to $943 million, offer the surface-level explanation.

However, the richer explanation lies in how that growth was achieved against a largely unchanged base of publishers. AppLovin experienced just a moderate drop in monthly active payers in its Apps business, but average revenue per monthly active payer grew to $52. Meanwhile, ad net revenue per install grew by effectively 49%, a good sign that the lift was accomplished on higher predictive accuracy and shorter conversion cycles rather than raw volume alone.

Management commentary validates this operating reality. CEO Adam Foroughi consistently emphasized that step-function increments in Axon's algorithmic accuracy, combined with reinforcement learning from billions of impressions, are fueling a virtuous cycle where the model becomes incrementally smarter with each spent dollar. This not only generates margin expansion but also protection against the seasonalities and churn risk that beset typical programmatic ad businesses. The historic volatility in Games businesses illustrates this distinction. In deciding to spin off its legacy Games segment in order to best focus on performance-based ad, AppLovin is refocusing its platform on this scalable ML advantage.

Early Signs of Stickier Web and E-commerce Expansion

AppLovin's revenue stream is said to be too concentrated on mobile games to be protected against regulatory changes, particularly changes in the tariffs or new payment structures. Yet, new disclosures and pilot outcomes provide a contrarian indication. The initial win in bringing on mid-market e-commerce clients indicates that the fundamental Axon architecture can be generalized outside of games. While web advertisers are still a small percentage of total addressable market penetration, the initial cohort's run-rate was still enough to contribute to the segment's record performance, even during an otherwise sluggish summer quarter.

This momentum was achieved despite a lean onboarding team of twenty people, which has been intentionally constrained while AppLovin finalizes its self-service dashboard. Once this tool launches more broadly, advertisers will gain full automation in campaign setup, budget allocation, and creative upload, a potential inflection point to unlock scale comparable to what the company achieved in gaming after rolling out Axon's second generation. Management expects a tipping point with more advertisers onboard; more data improves the model; boosting returns and retention means more data, which improves the model, which in turn drives better return on ad spend and deeper customer retention.

While the revenue contribution from non-gaming web spend is still nascent, its early churn metrics are telling. For web advertisers spending at least a mid six-figure annualized budget, churn has stayed below 3%, nearing gaming's near-zero advertiser attrition rate. This suggests that once Axon's predictive matching hits full stride in e-commerce and web-based performance, the company could replicate its stickiness in new verticals. Moreover, initiatives like generative AI for dynamic ad creative, already in pilot, could compress the time-to-value for advertisers and increase lifetime value per account.

Applovin's Cash Machine is Just Warming Up

AppLovin's free cash flow has increased from $388 million to $826 million in a period spanning a little more than four years, representing a more than 110% increase. This remarkable rate of expansion isn't merely due to top-line acceleration; it reflects good operating leverage with prudent capital stewardship and a highly efficient ad platform. Notably, share count was maintained relatively flat while still expanding FCF scale.

This reflects shareholder-friendly capital deployment. Where peers require significant headcount or services investment to achieve margin acceleration, AppLovin's automation-centric strategy appears to be yielding margin gains. Consistency in growth amidst variable macroenvironments reflects a strong asset-light model with increasing reinvestment headroom.

Further, operating leverage exists and looks durable. AppLovin's lean economics, most prominently automation-focused as opposed to people-based scale for services like campaign management, content moderation, or localized scale-out, has allowed the business to attain industry-best flow-through from incremental revenue. Recent guidance suggests that almost every new revenue dollar is expected to flow through directly as adjusted EBITDA. This operating pattern is a dramatic contrast to most ad-tech peers that continue to utilize expensive human-intensive services to manage campaigns, for content moderation, or localized scale-out.

AppLovin reconfirmed that discipline with its latest shareholder letter, reiterating that automation before hiring is one of its founding principles. By keeping human involvement minimal and scaling through AI agents, AppLovin avoids the margin drag that tends to accompany new segment penetration. Its approach to bringing that same formula to connected TV, even if still in early testing phases, would diversify revenue further while bringing that same DNA of performance that lies behind its currently leading margins in mobile games.

Lastly, one point of concern is that stock-based compensation remains meaningful despite share buybacks taking most dilution out. The Apps segment, though now deprioritized, still delivers lumpy low-teens top-line growth with good margins that need to be watched for any post-sale disruption after completion of transition to Tripledot Studios. However, the strategic divestment from a capital-intensive, more volatile pipeline of games to a more stable ad-based business model built on proven performance metrics eliminates a main source of quarterly lumpiness.

Rule of 40? APP's Playing a Bigger Game

Despite its stock's meteoric rise in value over the last couple of years, the market perhaps still underestimates AppLovin's cash flow strength compared to peer ad-tech companies. The firm now boasts free cash flow margins securely in excess of 40% with its adjusted EBITDA margin in the high fifties. The ad unit frequently enjoys segment margins in excess of 75%, levels most in the industry achieve based on much higher people costs and less effective reinvestment.

Framed from both a valuation and growth perspective, especially one that values the advertising segment using sustainable price-to-free-cash-flow multiples, APP deserves to trade in line with high-multiple, Rule of 40 software companies. Its structural advantage lies in a feedback loop where each dollar spent trains the model to deliver smarter targeting and stronger returns, creating a cash-generating engine that's hard to replicate.

While skepticism remains around its push into web and e-commerce, that may prove to be the next market mispricing. Once the self-serve dashboard sees broader adoption and starts compounding advertiser spend, AppLovin's broader ad platform narrative could quickly rerate.

Risk Factors: Scaling Pains, Competitive Encroachment, and Regulatory Shadows

Of course, there are still a few real risks investors should keep in mind. First off, a leap from mobile game commerce to larger web and networked television isn't necessarily set to stretch to a comparable forecasting potential. Early cohort abandonment rates hold promise, while larger consideration period advertisers or higher dollar value products might not realize a comparable conversion ROI that Axon produces for voluntary app installs.

Second, competitive risk exists. The same generative AI and dynamic creative optimization AppLovin is testing could be taken up by larger players in the walled garden who have much deeper first-party data moats. Meta, Google, and Amazon are not sitting idle. The question is whether AppLovin's independence and performance mentality is going to grow more popular with mid-market advertisers seeking clear incrementality in a world without cookies.

Third, overhangs from authorities in the guise of volatile app-store fees, small new e-commerce merchant tariffs, or new privacy guidelines might introduce friction in advertiser spending. Commentary around the comparatively diversified mid-market web exposure for the company suggests this risk is more likely to be spread out than devastating in impact, but investors would still need to be alert to churn happening on the merchant level or compression for cost-per-action patterns.

Final Take for Investors

Skeptics who dismiss AppLovin as just another ad-tech company riding a macro rebound may be missing the bigger picture. Beneath the headline numbers, AppLovin is quietly building a smarter, more efficient business with lean reinvestment and a growing competitive edge. The coming year will test whether Axon's learning loop can extend its dominance from gaming into broader e-commerce and connected TV.

data.title: 4 Calls For Trump's Pharmaceutical Tariffs
data.description:
By Diederik Stadig

1. US consumers will be hurt the most by the incoming tariffs

We expect the Trump administration to announce a tariff on pharmaceuticals before 1 August. If the instituted tariff is 25%, we believe prices for US consumers will gradually rise by 10-14%, as stockpiled medication slowly runs out.

This would mean that the price of commonly prescribed generic heart medication that sells for roughly $0.82 per pill would increase to $0.94 per pill. The same tariff would cause more complex and expensive generic cancer drugs to increase in price by $8,000-10,000 for a 24-week treatment. Both these calculations assume a 30% markup for the importing party and a 20% price hike on the exported medicine.

In this scenario, some tariff costs could be incurred by the exporter, which may not happen given how thin margins are for cheaper, generic drugs. At 25%, we expect there to be relatively few producers that avoid the US market altogether, so drug shortages will increase somewhat, but they should not spike.

It's therefore clear that a tariff would hurt consumers most of all, as they would feel the inflationary effect of tariffs directly when paying for prescriptions at the pharmacy and indirectly through higher insurance premiums. Lower-income households and the elderly, who rely on fixed incomes, would be disproportionately affected, as prescription drugs constitute a larger portion of their expenses.

2. Branded manufacturers will continue spending, while generic manufacturers stay put

On 22 July, AstraZeneca (AZN) became the latest manufacturer of branded pharmaceuticals to announce increased investment in the US. The threat of tariffs has caused branded pharma companies to commit to more than $250bn in US investment over the next few years. We expect increasing investment in the US to continue for several reasons.

First, the US is responsible for more than 40% of global pharmaceutical sales, which means that branded pharma companies need to be present in the US. Second, American politicians want more control over pharmaceutical supply chains, which means both Democrats and Republicans want to stimulate medicine production on American soil. Third, fiscal policy has been tightened in smaller European economies over the past few years following Covid spending and international tax cooperation. These three things combined, in addition to the short time to market in the US, make the US a logical place to invest for branded pharma companies.

By comparison, generic companies will not increase their US investments. Their margins are, generally speaking, half the size of those of branded manufacturers, and they lack the pricing power that branded pharma companies have. In addition, the economies of scale in countries like India, for example, are not achievable in the US. Moreover, investment horizons for new manufacturing facilities are three to 10 years, at which point the political landscape could look very different. If very high tariffs do come into force, generic pharma companies will likely avoid the US market, leading to drug shortages.

3. A 200% tariff will not happen

Speaking of high tariffs, we do not expect a 200% blanket tariff (or anything close to it) to materialise, because the effects would be disastrous: US drug prices and shortages would soar. The most important drugs that would become scarce are low-cost, low-margin, generic and life-saving medications, such as common antibiotics (such as doxycycline), sterile injectables (like injectable chemotherapy agents), chronic medications (e.g. hydrocortisone and blood pressure medication), biosimilars and insulin. The US produces a relatively small share of these medications domestically.

In terms of prices, a 200% tariff on pharmaceutical imports could significantly increase US inflation. Prescription drugs make up 1.25% of CPI, and by extrapolating our estimated price effect of a 25% tariff, a 200% rise could increase CPI by roughly one percentage point – particularly when considering that pharmaceuticals are essential goods with inelastic demand, meaning price hikes are less likely to suppress consumer purchase volumes in the short term. Thus, much of the tariff cost would show up as pure price inflation rather than reduced consumption.

Beyond the direct effect on drug prices, there are secondary inflation effects to consider. If hospitals and clinics face higher costs for medications (e.g. chemotherapy drugs, surgical anaesthetics, etc.), they may raise the prices of medical services to compensate. This would feed into hospital services and overall healthcare inflation. Additionally, higher drug costs would increase health insurance costs, which are indirectly captured in CPI.

4. CDMOs and logistics providers will benefit

Although the effects of tariffs are negative for the American consumer, two parts of the pharmaceutical value chain will benefit: Contract Development Manufacturing Organisations (CDMOs) and logistics providers. CDMOs offer flexible manufacturing capacity that is in high demand right now because of the uncertainty surrounding tariffs. We expect this trend to continue and to see increasing M&A, investment and growth in this space over the next few months and years.

Logistics providers are also set to benefit, as enabling stockpiling is a key mitigation strategy for various pharma companies. As a result, these two subsectors are poised to play a critical role in the resilience of pharmaceutical supply chains.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Equinor: Earn A Double-Digit Yield
data.description:
Equinor (NYSE:EQNR), the Norwegian oil company, is one of the largest in the world, producing more than 2 million barrels a day of oil equivalent. The company is also a major renewables investor. Despite that, the company sits at a market cap of less than $70 billion.

We last recommended the company as a Strong Buy, and we continue to recommend investing, based on the company's assets and strong renewables, but now as a buy given the company's appreciation since our last article.

Equinor 2Q 2025 Results

The company had strong operational performance in the quarter, with $9.3 billion in YTD CFFO after-tax.

The company operates in a high tax environment where roughly 50% of its cash flow goes to taxes (making up a staggering 20% of Norway's annual government budget). The company has continued to deliver on key production milestones, and the massive Empire Wind 1 project is back on track after substantial setbacks earlier in Trump's term.

The company has maintained its dividend policy of roughly 6% annualized while continuing to buy back shares, and it continues to guide for $9 billion in 2025 capital distributions, or a 12-13% yield.

Equinor Equity Production

The company saw >2% in oil and gas production growth for the quarter and 26% in renewable production growth.

For perspective on rates, the company's Empire Wind 1 project in New York is expected to earn roughly $155/mWh based on contracts with the state. Using that valuation for renewables puts total revenue at roughly $120 million for the quarter, or 1.3% of the company's CFFO. That shows that while renewables are growing, they're still a small part of the company.

The company's oil and gas production saw >2% growth, supported by strong natural gas production. This is quite impressive given $3.5 billion of divestments in the quarter, as the company has continued to see especially strong performance in its U.S. operations, where it is becoming a growing player.

Equinor Cash Flow Breakdown

From a cash flow perspective, the company continues to work towards maintaining guidance and driving strong returns.

The company had $9.2 billion in CFFO in 2Q 2025, but the volatility of tax obligations means it spent more than $7 billion in tax for the same period. Using YTD numbers, which provide a much better estimate, the company had $19.8 billion in CFFO and paid $10.5 billion in taxes, leaving $9.3 billion. Annualized, that's ~$18.6 billion.

The company invested $6.5 billion, or roughly half of its guided annual capex of $13 billion, leaving the company with ~$3.3 billion. It spent ~10% more than that on capital distributions, which it can comfortably afford with its net debt ratio. And it has the cash (almost $24 billion) to continue substantial shareholder returns going forward.

Equinor Outlook and Returns

The company remains committed to its growth and its ability to continue generating shareholder returns.

The company had $13 billion in organic capex guidance, which it expects to be able to comfortably make. That's roughly 20% of its market cap, and it's expected to result in 4% production, growth, which is quite impressive. The company's share buybacks mean that production per share will go even higher.

The company is still guiding to $9 billion in capital distributions, although some will come from the company's cash position; it can comfortably afford it. At the company's current share prices, we think that repurchasing shares is the right decision here.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. Equinor is still, fundamentally, an oil and natural gas company reliant on higher natural gas prices in Europe and high global oil prices. A prolonged downturn along with long-term risk as oil and gas get replaced could hurt the company's ability to continue providing shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Equinor, despite being one of the largest international oil and gas producers, has a market cap of less than $70 billion. The company has continued to maintain strong production of more than 2 million barrels/day with ~50% of it being gas, which is in high demand in Europe. The company is also building up a strong renewables portfolio.

The company continues to have expensive tax rates, but going into 2H of the year, they're expected to be much more manageable. That's expected to be caused by the tax now being broken into 5 installments with 2x in the upcoming quarter (~$4 billion worth). Reliable cash flow will enable less volatile shareholder returns.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.
